knowledge.statement: The test pyramid model recommends having many unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and even fewer end-to-end tests at the top. Higher-level tests are slower and more brittle; lower-level tests provide faster feedback.
Unit tests verify the behaviour of a single unit of code (a function, class, or module) in isolation. Dependencies are replaced with fakes or mocks. They run fast and pinpoint failures precisely.

Integration tests verify that two or more components work correctly together. They may use real databases, queues, or external services, making them slower than unit tests but more representative of production behaviour.
End-to-end (E2E) tests exercise the complete system from the user interface to the database. They catch integration and regression bugs but are slow to run, fragile, and expensive to maintain.

Test-Driven Development (TDD) is a development practice where a failing test is written before the production code that makes it pass. The cycle is Red (write failing test) → Green (minimal code to pass) → Refactor.

Behaviour-Driven Development (BDD) extends TDD by expressing tests in natural language that describes the expected behaviour from the user's perspective. Given-When-Then format bridges the gap between technical and non-technical stakeholders.

A mock is a test double that records calls and can verify that expected interactions occurred. A stub returns predefined values to isolate the code under test. A spy wraps the real object and records interactions without replacing behaviour.
A fake is a working implementation of a dependency that is simpler than the real one, such as an in-memory database. Fakes are used for integration tests where full mocking is impractical.

Test fixtures provide a fixed, known state for tests. They set up the conditions necessary for a test to run and are torn down afterward. Shared fixtures speed up test suites but can cause tests to interfere with each other.

The Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern structures tests into three sections: set up the preconditions, perform the action being tested, and verify the expected outcome. It makes tests readable and consistent.
Each test should be independent and not rely on the execution order of other tests. Tests that depend on shared state or ordering are fragile and produce misleading failures when run in isolation.

Test coverage measures the percentage of production code executed by tests. High coverage does not guarantee correctness, but low coverage indicates blind spots. Aim for high coverage of critical paths, not 100% line coverage as a goal.

Mutation testing evaluates test quality by introducing small changes (mutations) to production code and verifying that tests fail. A high mutation score means tests are good at detecting bugs; surviving mutants indicate weak tests.
Property-based testing generates a large number of random inputs and verifies that a property (invariant) holds for all of them. It is more thorough than example-based testing for finding edge cases.

Contract testing verifies that a consumer's expectations of a provider's API are met, and that the provider fulfils all known consumer contracts. It enables teams to deploy independently without expensive E2E tests for every change.

Consumer-Driven Contract testing (CDC) has each consumer define the contract (expectations) it has on a provider. The provider runs all consumer contracts in CI to ensure it does not break any consumer.

Snapshot testing captures the serialised output of a component (HTML, JSON, rendered UI) and fails when the output changes. It is fast to write but can lead to accepting regressions if snapshots are updated without review.

Load testing measures how a system performs under an expected load (number of users or requests per second). It identifies throughput limits and latency degradation before they affect production users.
Stress testing pushes a system beyond its normal operating capacity to find its breaking point and observe how it fails. The goal is to understand failure modes, not just measure normal performance.
Soak (endurance) testing runs a system at sustained load for an extended period to detect memory leaks, connection pool exhaustion, and other issues that only appear after prolonged operation.

Chaos engineering intentionally introduces failures into a production or staging system to verify resilience. It uncovers weaknesses that are not apparent from code review or unit tests, such as unhandled network timeouts.
A/B testing exposes different versions of a feature to different user segments and measures which performs better against a defined metric. It enables data-driven product decisions.

Regression testing re-runs existing tests after a code change to ensure that previously working functionality has not been broken. Automated regression suites are the primary safeguard for continuous delivery.

Smoke testing runs a minimal set of tests after a deployment to verify that the most critical features work before running the full test suite. It provides rapid feedback and can abort further testing early if a critical feature is broken.

Acceptance testing verifies that a system meets business requirements from the user's perspective. It is typically the final gate before a release and may be performed by QA, product owners, or automated acceptance frameworks.

Exploratory testing is simultaneous learning, test design, and execution. A tester explores the system without predefined scripts, using insights from each interaction to guide the next. It complements scripted tests by finding unexpected issues.

The Test Doubles principle (Meszaros) categorises substitutes into dummies, stubs, spies, mocks, and fakes based on their purpose. Using the right type minimises test fragility and makes intent clear.

Dependency injection makes code testable by allowing test code to inject fakes or mocks in place of real dependencies. Code that creates its own dependencies with new or singletons is hard to test in isolation.

A test should verify one thing. Tests that assert multiple unrelated behaviours are harder to understand and produce ambiguous failure messages. One assertion per test is a guideline, not a strict rule.
Test names should describe the behaviour being tested in plain language, not the method name. A good name reads like a sentence: given_when_then or should_do_something_when_condition.
Test data should be as minimal as possible. Only the data relevant to the specific test should be set up. Irrelevant data adds noise, makes tests longer, and obscures the intent.

The Test Hermetic principle states that a test should have no external dependencies — no real databases, networks, or file systems — unless explicitly required. Hermetic tests are fast, reliable, and runnable anywhere.

Flaky tests pass sometimes and fail others without code changes. They erode confidence in the test suite. Flakiness is usually caused by time-dependent behaviour, shared mutable state, or undeterministic ordering.

Parallelising the test suite reduces feedback time. Tests must be independent (no shared mutable state) to run in parallel safely. Database tests often need per-test transaction rollback or isolated schemas.
Test pyramid inversion (too many E2E, too few unit tests) is an anti-pattern called the ice cream cone. It produces slow, brittle suites that make refactoring dangerous and CI pipelines unusable.
API testing verifies the functionality, reliability, and performance of APIs independently of the UI. It is faster than E2E testing and a key part of microservices testing strategy.

Security testing (DAST, SAST) should be part of the CI/CD pipeline. SAST analyses source code for vulnerabilities statically; DAST tests the running application from the outside. Both catch different classes of vulnerabilities.

The Test-First mindset — writing tests before implementation — forces you to think about the interface and expected behaviour before writing code. Even outside strict TDD, writing tests early improves design.
Continuous testing runs the test suite automatically on every commit. Fast feedback from continuous testing reduces the cost of fixing bugs, which grows exponentially the later a bug is discovered.

Test environments should mirror production as closely as possible. Differences between environments (OS, library versions, data volumes) are a common source of bugs that pass all tests but fail in production.
Parameterised tests run the same test logic with multiple sets of inputs, reducing duplication. They are preferable to duplicating tests for similar cases and make it easy to add new edge cases.

Test after development has the highest risk of tests being biased toward the implementation, low coverage of edge cases, and being skipped under time pressure. TDD or test-first approaches mitigate these risks.

The Humble Object pattern extracts logic that is difficult to test into a separate object, leaving behind a thin, humble shell that is trivially tested or untested. UI rendering code is often separated from presentation logic this way.

Golden master (approval) testing captures the output of a system at a known-good state and uses it as the expected output for future tests. It is useful for legacy code without tests and for detecting unintended output changes.

Testability is a first-class design concern. Code that is hard to test is often also poorly designed — it usually has high coupling, hidden dependencies, or too many responsibilities. Difficulty testing is a design signal.

Test quarantine temporarily disables flaky tests by tagging them so they do not block CI. Quarantined tests should be tracked and fixed promptly; an accumulating quarantine backlog is a serious quality risk.
Branch coverage ensures tests exercise both the true and false branches of every conditional. It is a more meaningful metric than line coverage for detecting untested logic.

Pact is a popular consumer-driven contract testing framework that stores contracts as JSON files (pacts) and provides a broker to share them between services. It verifies contracts independently on both sides of an integration.

The Testing Trophy (Kent C. Dodds) de-emphasises unit tests in favour of integration tests, arguing that integration tests give the best return on investment by testing real interactions without the full cost of E2E tests.

When testing async code, tests must await completion of all asynchronous work before asserting outcomes. Asserting before async operations complete leads to false positives — tests that pass for the wrong reason.

Database tests should use transactions that are rolled back after each test, or dedicated per-test schemas. This ensures tests are independent and do not leave persistent side effects that affect other tests.
Test data factories (e.g. factory_boy, FactoryGirl) generate test objects with sensible defaults that tests can override. They reduce boilerplate and make test setup intent-revealing.

Performance benchmarks should be tracked over time and fail CI when they regress beyond a threshold. Tracking performance alongside functional tests prevents gradual performance degradation from going unnoticed.
